source.title: Creating a more robust 5-hydroxymethylfurfural oxidase by combining computational predictions with a novel effective library design

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The research aims to engineer a more robust 5-hydroxymethylfurfural oxidase (HMFO) for industrial FDCA production. Wild-type HMFO has low thermostability (Tm_app = 48.5 °C) and limited activity in the final oxidation step. The goal is to enhance thermostability while maintaining activity, using computational predictions (FRESCO) to identify stabilizing mutations and a novel Golden Gate gene shuffling method to efficiently combine them. The final variant should tolerate harsh conditions and serve as a template for further activity improvements.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: HMFO oxidizes HMF to FDCA in three steps. To increase robustness for biotech applications, thermostability was enhanced using FRESCO, a computational method. A new gene shuffling approach rapidly combined beneficial mutations, yielding a thermostable mutant that outperforms wild-type in FDCA production.
process_signals: Problem: low robustness of HMFO for industrial use.
Approach: FRESCO for computational prediction of stabilizing mutations.
Innovation: new gene shuffling to combine mutations efficiently.
Outcome: thermostable HMFO variant with improved FDCA production.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: FDCA is a bio-based polymer building block from carbohydrates. HMFO from Methylovorus sp. converts HMF to FDCA but has modest stability and inefficient last step. Previous mutant V367R-W466F improved activity but reduced thermostability. Need for robust variant for industrial conditions. FRESCO method previously validated for enzyme stabilization.
process_signals: Motivation: FDCA as renewable polymer precursor.
Limitation: HMFO low thermostability and poor FFA oxidation.
Prior work: V367R-W466F mutant improved activity but destabilized enzyme.
Constraint: need to maintain activity while improving stability.
Hypothesis: FRESCO can predict stabilizing mutations; shuffling can combine them.
failures_or_limits: V367R-W466F mutant had higher activity but lower thermostability.

section_gists.heading: Results and discussion
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Computational screening
section_gists.gist: In silico phase of FRESCO: all point mutations (excluding residues within 5 Å of FAD) modeled on HMFO structure (PDB:4UDQ). Free energy predictions (ΔΔG_Fold) using FoldX and Rosetta; variants with ΔΔG_Fold > -5 kJ/mol discarded, reducing 9044 to 744. MD simulations and visual inspection for stability features (flexibility, hydrophobic exposure, H-bonds) selected 140 mutants for experimental testing.
process_signals: Hypothesis: mutations far from active site avoid activity loss.
Selection criteria: ΔΔG_Fold < -5 kJ/mol, favorable MD features.
Design space: 9044 possible mutations reduced to 140.
methods: FoldX and Rosetta for ΔΔG_Fold prediction
metrics: Number of variants after each filter
failures_or_limits: DDD algorithm not used to avoid expression complications.

section_gists.heading: Screening of single mutants
section_gists.gist: 140 mutations introduced by PCR, expressed in 96-well plates. Thermostability measured by ThermoFAD assay (Tm_app) using cell extracts and purified enzymes. 17 mutations showed significant positive ΔTm_app. ThermoFAD on cell extracts was consistent with purified enzymes, enabling high-throughput screening.
process_signals: Validation: ThermoFAD on cell extracts is reliable.
Success: 17 stabilizing mutations identified.
Data: ΔTm_app values for each mutant (Fig. 1).
failures_or_limits: Only 17 out of 140 predicted mutations were stabilizing.

section_gists.heading: Golden Gate gene shuffling
section_gists.gist: To combine beneficial mutations, a novel Golden Gate gene shuffling method was developed. Eight top mutations (I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, A435E) selected based on ΔTm_app and activity (vanillyl alcohol assay). I73V and H74Y combined additively (ΔTm_app +6 °C). Shuffling designed for 7 sites (128 combinations) using one-pot BsaI restriction-ligation with two donor vectors (WT and 8-fold mutant) and acceptor vector. Library analysis: 97% correct restriction pattern, 65% shuffling efficiency.
process_signals: Challenge: non-additivity of mutations.
Solution: random shuffling to test all combinations.
Design: synthetic genes with BsaI sites for scarless ligation.
Efficiency: 65% non-redundant shuffled sequences.
failures_or_limits: Q187E later found to negatively affect activity.

section_gists.heading: Shuffled library screening
section_gists.gist: Library screened (3.5x oversampling) for Tm_app and HMF activity. 8xHMFO (all 8 mutations) had highest Tm_app (+13 °C) but Q187E reduced activity. Best variant: 7xHMFO (without Q187E) with Tm_app = 60.5 °C, kcat/Km comparable to WT. 7xHMFO showed enhanced stability at 40 °C (50% activity after 10 days vs WT lost in 2 days) and higher cosolvent tolerance (dioxane, methanol, DMSO, ethanol).
process_signals: Trade-off: Q187E increases stability but decreases activity.
Selection: 7xHMFO as optimal balance.
Validation: improved operational stability and solvent tolerance.
failures_or_limits: Some combinations may not have been additive.

section_gists.heading: Engineering of thermostable HMFO variant for FDCA production
section_gists.gist: Active-site mutations V367R and W466F introduced into 7xHMFO to improve FDCA production. Variants: 8AxHMFO (7x + W466F) and 8BxHMFO (7x + V367R, W466F). Both had reduced Tm_app but still higher than WT. 8BxHMFO showed best FDCA production: >95% conversion in 9 h at 40 °C, vs <50% for double mutant at 25 °C. Higher conversion attributed to improved operational stability despite lower kcat/Km.
process_signals: Trade-off: activity mutations reduce stability but template remains robust.
Success: 8BxHMFO superior biocatalyst for FDCA.
Mechanism: operational stability enables longer reactions.
failures_or_limits: 8BxHMFO has lower kcat/Km than double mutant.
Stability decrease upon introducing activity mutations.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Golden Gate gene shuffling efficiently combined stabilizing mutations, yielding 7xHMFO with +12 °C Tm_app and improved cosolvent tolerance. 7xHMFO served as template for active-site mutations, resulting in 8BxHMFO with superior FDCA production. Method advances FRESCO protocol.
process_signals: Achievement: rapid identification of optimal mutation combination.
Impact: thermostable template for further engineering.
Limitation: not explicitly stated, but need for further optimization implied.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Materials
section_gists.gist: All materials from Sigma-Aldrich unless specified.

section_gists.heading: Computational methods
section_gists.gist: FRESCO protocol details: used HMFO structure 4UDQ, excluded residues <5 Å from FAD. ΔΔG_Fold calculated with FoldX (5 runs) and Rosetta (row-3 protocol). Mutations with ΔΔG_Fold < -5 kJ/mol subjected to MD simulations in Yasara. Visual inspection of averaged structures for flexibility, H-bonds, hydrophobic exposure to select final set.
process_signals: Specific computational tools and parameters.
Selection criteria: energy threshold and structural features.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Computational predictions (FRESCO) can identify stabilizing mutations in HMFO without compromising activity by avoiding active-site residues.
A novel Golden Gate gene shuffling method can efficiently combine multiple stabilizing mutations to overcome non-additivity and rapidly identify optimal variants.
A thermostable HMFO variant can serve as a robust scaffold for introducing activity-enhancing mutations, leading to improved FDCA production under operational conditions.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What were the specific ΔΔG_Fold values for the selected 17 mutations?
What were the exact activity changes for single mutants on HMF?
How was the shuffling library exactly constructed (vector maps, overhang sequences)?
What were the Tm_app and activity data for all 128 combinations?
What were the side products in FDCA conversion?
What are the limitations in terms of scale-up and industrial conditions?

events.summary: HMFO has modest stability and inefficient last oxidation step, limiting industrial application.
FRESCO computational method can predict stabilizing mutations in HMFO.
All point mutations (excluding residues within 5 Å of FAD) were modeled on HMFO structure (PDB:4UDQ) for free energy predictions.
A novel Golden Gate gene shuffling method was developed to combine beneficial mutations efficiently.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, background, and initial results including computational screening and single mutant screening, establishing the problem, hypothesis, design, computation, experiment, and analysis events.

events.summary: Designed a Golden Gate gene shuffling method to combine 7 stabilizing mutations into all 128 possible combinations.
Designed synthetic genes with BsaI sites flanking modules to enable scarless ligation and random shuffling.
Performed one-pot Golden Gate cloning to generate shuffled library and analyzed efficiency.
Screened shuffled library for thermostability and HMF activity, identifying 8xHMFO as most thermostable but Q187E detrimental to activity.
Validated 7xHMFO stability: retained 50% activity after 10 days at 40 °C, improved cosolvent tolerance.
Introduced active-site mutations V367R and W466F into 7xHMFO to improve FDCA production, creating 8BxHMFO.
8BxHMFO achieved >95% FDCA conversion in 9 h at 40 °C, outperforming WT and double mutant due to improved operational stability.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers the design and validation of the Golden Gate gene shuffling method, screening of the shuffled library, selection of 7xHMFO, and engineering of 8BxHMFO for FDCA production. All events are directly supported by quotes.

events.summary: Golden Gate gene shuffling method designed using BsaI restriction-ligation with two donor vectors (WT and 8-fold mutant) and an acceptor vector.
Restriction-ligation thermocycler program: 50 cycles of 37°C for 5 min and 16°C for 10 min, final digestion at 50°C for 10 min, inactivation at 80°C for 10 min.
Transformation of restriction-ligation reaction into chemically competent NEB 10β cells and plating on ampicillin LB agar plates.
Verification of hmfo variants by colony PCR for insert size and sequencing.
Small-scale expression of single point variants in E. coli BL21(DE3) with pET SUMO-HMFO vector, induced with IPTG at OD600 2.0, overnight at 24°C.
Small-scale expression of multi-site variants in E. coli NEB 10β with pBAD SUMO-HMFO vector, induced with L-arabinose at OD600 2.0, overnight at 24°C.
Small-scale purification using Ni-Sepharose resin and desalting with PD MultiTrap G-25 plates.
Thermostability assay using ThermoFAD method on cell-free extract or purified enzyme to determine melting temperature.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for Golden Gate shuffling, expression, purification, and thermostability assay, which are core to the experimental validation of the designed variants.

evidence_bank.quote: the enzyme displays only a modest stability
it is rather inefficient in the last step
we decided to enhance its thermostability using the recently developed FRESCO method: a computational approach to identify thermostabilizing mutations in a protein structure
all possible point mutations were modeled (excluding residues that are within 5 Å from the FAD cofactor)
we now developed a new and facile gene shuffling approach to rapidly combine stabilizing mutations in a one-pot reaction
The aim of the gene shuffling approach was to create a library of all the possible combinations (128) of the selected 7 sites
The Golden Gate gene shuffling which we developed for the FRESCO strategy is a one-pot reaction that involves only three designed vectors and a single restriction-ligation reaction
The first step was the design of two synthetic genes, one wild-type version and one with the selected 7 mutations; each containing 8 BsaI restriction sites that flank the 7 gene modules
the four overhang nucleotides at the end of each module are replicated at the beginning of the following module to allow a scarless ligation
The results showed that 97% of the colonies contained the correct restriction pattern and the shuffling efficiency was 65%
the 8xHMFO (I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, and A435E) was the most thermostable variant with an improvement of 13 °C compared with the WT
the mutation Q187E had a negative effect on the activity
the thermostable variant retained 50% of its activity even after 10 days of incubation at 40 °C
The 7xHMFO showed a much higher tolerance towards four commonly used cosolvents
we introduced two mutations (V367R and W466F) in the active site
At this temperature, only the 8BxHMFO mutant performed well (> 95% conversion in only 9 h)
This indicates that the higher conversion is largely due to an improved operational stability
A derivative of pBAD SUMO vector designed with BsaI cutting site 5'-TGGTngagacc and ggtctcnCTTG-3') was used as receiving vector.
The restriction-ligation reaction was set up in 20 $\mu$ L volume with the components: pBAD SUMO 3.75 ng $\mu$ L $^{-1}$ , pUC57wt 2.5 ng $\mu$ L $^{-1}$ , pUC578x-mutant 2.5 ng $\mu$ L $^{-1}$

the thermocycler program was: incubation at 37 °C for 5 min and at 16 °C for 10 min repeated 50 times, followed by a final incubation at 50 °C for 10 min (final digestion) and at 80 °C for 10 min (enzyme inactivation).
The restriction-ligation reaction (5 $\mu$ L) were used to transform 100 $\mu$ L of chemically competent NEB 10 $\beta$ cells plated on 100 $\mu$ g mL $^{-1}$ ampicillin LB agar plates.
The hmfo variants were verified first by colony PCR (DreamTaq Green PCR Master Mix, Thermo Fisher) to determine the inserts size and then by sequencing.
The single point variants were expressed in E. coli BL21(DE3) cells with the pET SUMO-HMFO vector.
The multi-site variants were expressed with the pBAD SUMO-HMFO vector in E. coli NEB 10 $\beta$ cells
mixed with 100 μL of pre-equilibrated Ni-Sepharose resin (GE-Healthcare) for 15 min using an AcroPrep Advance 1 mL 96-well plate (Pall).
The eluate was desalted in 50 mM phosphate buffer pH 8.0 using PD MultiTrap G-25 plates (GE-Healthcare).

The melting temperature of HMFO cell-free extract or purified variants was tested by the ThermoFAD method, which allows to determine the unfolding temperature based on the release of the flavin cofactor

The conversion performed by HMFO WT, HMFO V367R-W466F, HMFO I73V-H74Y-G356H-V367L-T414K-A419Y-A435E (7xHMFO), HMFO I73V-H74Y-G356H-V367L-T414K-A419Y-A435E-W466F (8AxHMFO), HMFO I73V-H74Y-G356H-V367R-T414K-A419Y-A435E-W466F (8BxHMFO), using 5.0 mM 5-(hydroxymethyl)furfural as a substrate were carried out at 25 or 40 °C, with shaking at 1000 rpm.
WT (filled triangle), V367R W466F HMFO (filled circle), 8BxHMFO (I73V-H74Y, G356H, V367R, T414K, A419Y, A435E, and W466F) (filled square).

The results of the ThermoFAD analysis showed that the 8xHMFO (I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, and A435E) was the most thermostable variant with an improvement of 13 °C compared with the WT ( $T_{m}^{app} = 48.5$ °C).

Therefore, the best multiple mutant was considered to be the 7xHMFO mutant (I73V, H74Y, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, and A435E) with a $T_{m}^{app}$ of 60.5 °C and $k_{cat}$ and $K_{m}$ values comparable with the wild-type enzyme (Table 1) [8].
Since V367 had already been mutated in the 7xHMFO variant (V367L), we included in the analysis also another 8xHMFO variant with only the W466F mutation.

To further tune the 7xHMFO variant towards conversion of HMF into FDCA, we introduced two mutations (V367R and W466F) in the active site that have been shown to be beneficial for FDCA production from HMF [11].
The mutant V367R-W466F HMFO was found to be the best variant for FDCA production with a $k_{cat}/K_{m}$ value for FFA conversion > 1000-fold higher than for the wild-type enzyme $[11]$ .

To determine whether the thermostable variants are more potent biocatalysts than the previously described wild-type and/or V367R W466F HMFO mutant, we tested the conversion rates using HMF as a substrate.

The gene single point variants, the double mutant I73V H74Y, the 8AxHMFO, and the 8BxHMFO were obtained from pET SUMO-HMFO or pBAD SUMO-7xHMFO by whole-plasmid PCR with PfuUltra II Hotstart PCR Master Mix (Agilent).
Before performing gene shuffling, the mutations I73V and H74Y, being neighboring residues, were combined to verify whether they display an additive effect.

Yet, an alignment of the best 9 multi-site mutants that presented an higher $T_{m}^{app}$ together with a preserved ability to oxidize HMF demonstrated that the mutation Q187E had a negative effect on the activity (Additional file 3: Table S3).

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Engineering a more robust 5-hydroxymethylfurfural oxidase (HMFO) for industrial FDCA production by enhancing thermostability while maintaining activity.

canonical_events.summary: Wild-type HMFO has low thermostability (Tm_app = 48.5 °C) and inefficient final oxidation step, limiting industrial application.
FRESCO computational method can predict stabilizing mutations in HMFO without compromising activity by avoiding active-site residues.

In silico screening: all point mutations (excluding residues within 5 Å of FAD) modeled on HMFO structure (PDB:4UDQ); free energy predictions (FoldX, Rosetta) filtered 9044 mutations to 744; MD simulations and visual inspection selected 140 for experimental testing.
Experimental screening of 140 single mutants: expressed in E. coli, thermostability measured by ThermoFAD assay; 17 mutations showed significant positive ΔTm_app.
Selection of top 8 mutations (I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, A435E) based on ΔTm_app and activity (vanillyl alcohol assay); I73V and H74Y combined additively (ΔTm_app +6 °C).

Development of a novel Golden Gate gene shuffling method to combine 7 mutation sites (128 combinations) using one-pot BsaI restriction-ligation with two donor vectors (WT and 8-fold mutant) and an acceptor vector.
Generation and analysis of shuffled library: 97% correct restriction pattern, 65% shuffling efficiency.
Screening of shuffled library (3.5x oversampling) for thermostability and HMF activity; 8xHMFO (all 8 mutations) had highest Tm_app (+13 °C) but Q187E reduced activity.
Identification of 7xHMFO (without Q187E) as best variant: Tm_app = 60.5 °C, kcat/Km comparable to WT.
Validation of 7xHMFO: retained 50% activity after 10 days at 40 °C (WT lost activity in 2 days), improved cosolvent tolerance (dioxane, methanol, DMSO, ethanol).

Introduction of active-site mutations V367R and W466F into 7xHMFO to improve FDCA production, creating 8AxHMFO (7x + W466F) and 8BxHMFO (7x + V367R, W466F); both had reduced Tm_app but still higher than WT.

8BxHMFO achieved >95% FDCA conversion in 9 h at 40 °C, outperforming WT and double mutant (V367R-W466F) due to improved operational stability, despite lower kcat/Km (0.1 s^-1 mM^-1 vs 2.2 s^-1 mM^-1 for double mutant).
Trade-off: activity-enhancing mutations (V367R, W466F) reduce thermostability; 8BxHMFO has lower catalytic efficiency than the double mutant, but operational stability compensates.
Golden Gate gene shuffling efficiently combined stabilizing mutations, advancing FRESCO protocol; 7xHMFO serves as robust scaffold for further engineering.

edges.reason: Problem of low thermostability motivates hypothesis to use FRESCO for stabilization.
Hypothesis leads to computational design to predict stabilizing mutations.
Computational predictions are experimentally tested for thermostability.
Experimental results allow selection of top mutations for combination.
Selected mutations motivate design of shuffling method to combine them.
Shuffling design is implemented to generate and analyze library.
Library is screened to identify thermostable variants.
Screening results lead to identification of best variant 7xHMFO.
7xHMFO is validated for operational stability and cosolvent tolerance.
Validated thermostable scaffold is used to introduce activity mutations.
Activity mutants are tested for FDCA production, showing improved performance.
High conversion achieved despite lower catalytic efficiency, highlighting trade-off.
Limitation informs that scaffold robustness is key, and method advances FRESCO.

must_cover_points: Problem: low thermostability and inefficient last step of HMFO.
Hypothesis: FRESCO can predict stabilizing mutations.
Experimental screening: 17 stabilizing single mutants identified.
Selection of top 8 mutations for shuffling.
Design of Golden Gate gene shuffling method.
Library generation and efficiency (97% correct, 65% shuffling).
Screening of shuffled library: 8xHMFO most stable but Q187E detrimental.
Validation of 7xHMFO: long-term stability and cosolvent tolerance.
Introduction of activity mutations V367R and W466F.
8BxHMFO achieves >95% FDCA conversion in 9 h at 40 °C.
Trade-off: lower kcat/Km but operational stability compensates.
